The parabolic mirror with its vertex at the origin (0,0) and a focal length of $f = 4$ meters follows the standard equation $x^2 = 4fy$. Substituting the focal length, the equation of the mirror is $x^2 = 16y$. To derive the path of a light ray striking the mirror at point $(x, y)$ and reflecting to the focus $(0, 4)$, we note that parallel rays from distant galaxies arrive horizontally. The slope of the tangent at $(x, y)$ is found by differentiating $y = x^2/16$, yielding $dy/dx = x/8$. The normal line at this point has a slope of $-8/x$. By the law of reflection, the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection relative to the normal. Since the incoming ray is horizontal, the reflected ray must pass through the focus $(0, 4)$. The equation of the reflected ray connecting $(x, y)$ and $(0, 4)$ is $Y - 4 = \frac{y-4}{x-0}(X - 0)$, which simplifies to $Y = \frac{x^2/16 - 4}{x}X + 4$.

Regarding the brightness calculation, the new coating increases the effective collecting area by 20%, meaning the new luminosity $L_2$ is 1.2 times the initial luminosity $L_1$. Using the magnitude formula $m_1 - m_2 = -2.5 \log_{10}(L_1/L_2)$, we substitute $L_1/L_2 = 1/1.2$. This gives $25 - m_2 = -2.5 \log_{10}(1/1.2) = 2.5 \log_{10}(1.2)$. Calculating the log value, $\log_{10}(1.2) \approx 0.07918$, so the magnitude difference is approximately $2.5 \times 0.07918 \approx 0.198$. The new apparent magnitude $m_2$ is $25 - 0.198 = 24.802$. The galaxy appears brighter by roughly 0.2 magnitudes. The data derived demonstrates that even small area gains
